title: Sequential Prompt Chains
concept: Piping the output of one prompt as the input to the next; extract, then summarize, then format.

exercises.prompt: Quick recap: why do we break a big prompt into a sequence of smaller prompts?

options.text: Because each small prompt has one narrow job, so it's easier to get right
Because smaller prompts always cost zero tokens
Because the AI can only read short sentences

exercises.explain: Decomposing a big task into smaller, focused prompts means each step has one clear job, which is easier for the model to do well and easier for you to debug.

cards.title: What if prompts could talk to each other?
cards.body: Imagine prompt #1 finishes its work and hands the result straight to prompt #2, who hands it to prompt #3. No copy-pasting, no middleman. That's a sequential prompt chain.

cards.title: Sequential Prompt Chain
cards.body: A sequential prompt chain is a series of prompts where the OUTPUT of one prompt becomes the INPUT of the next. Each step builds on the step before it, in order.

cards.title: Like a factory assembly line
cards.body: Station 1 cuts the fabric, station 2 sews it, station 3 adds buttons. Each worker only touches the item after the previous worker is done, and passes their result forward. Prompt chains work the same way, one output feeding the next input.

cards.title: Extract → Summarize → Format
cards.body: Prompt 1: extract the key facts from a messy news article. Prompt 2: take those facts and summarize them in 3 sentences. Prompt 3: take that summary and format it as a tweet with hashtags. Each prompt's output feeds the next prompt's input.

cards.title: Myth: One giant prompt does the same job
cards.body: You might think asking the AI to 'extract, summarize, and format' all in one prompt works just as well. In reality, piping outputs step by step usually gives cleaner, more reliable results because each step focuses on just one transformation.

cards.title: One Big Ask vs. A Chain of Small Asks
cards.body: See the difference between cramming everything into one prompt versus piping outputs through a sequence of focused prompts.

left.text: 'Extract facts, summarize, and format as a tweet' all at once — the model juggles three jobs and sometimes drops one.

right.text: Extract → output feeds Summarize → output feeds Format. Each step is clean and its result becomes the next step's material.

exercises.prompt: In a sequential prompt chain that does extract → summarize → format, what becomes the input to the 'summarize' prompt?

options.text: A blank prompt with no input

exercises.explain: In a sequential chain, each prompt's output feeds the next prompt's input in order. Since 'extract' runs first, its output becomes what 'summarize' works on next.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In a sequential prompt chain, the third prompt can run before the second prompt finishes.
exercises.explain: Sequential means order matters. The third prompt needs the second prompt's output as its input, so it must wait until the second prompt is done.

exercises.sentence: In a sequential prompt chain, the output of one prompt becomes the input to the next prompt.
exercises.explain: The core idea of a sequential chain is piping: one prompt's output feeds directly in as the next prompt's input.

exercises.prompt: A student describes their 3-step chain for turning a long research paper into a social post. Which description is the AI-generated one (sounds generic and vague, missing concrete piping details)?

options.text: Step 1: prompt extracts the 5 key findings as a bullet list. Step 2: that bullet list is fed into a prompt that writes a 3-sentence summary. Step 3: that summary is fed into a prompt that adds emojis and hashtags for a tweet.

This process leverages a multi-stage pipeline to holistically transform content through iterative refinement, ensuring optimal engagement and comprehensive coverage of key insights across various stages.
First I extract the stats from the abstract, then I pass just those stats into a summarizer prompt, then I pass that summary into a formatter prompt that shapes it into a tweet.

exercises.explain: The vague, buzzword-heavy option never says WHAT is piped between steps or what the output actually looks like — a telltale sign of generic AI filler text, unlike the other two which name concrete outputs (bullet list, stats, summary) being passed forward.

exercises.prompt: You're building a chain to turn a customer support email thread into a clean action item for your team: extract the customer's issue, summarize it, then format it as a JSON ticket. Which setup correctly uses sequential prompt chaining?

options.text: Run all three instructions (extract, summarize, format) inside a single prompt at once
Prompt 1 extracts the issue from the email thread; its output is fed as input to Prompt 2, which summarizes it; that summary is fed as input to Prompt 3, which formats it as JSON
Run Prompt 3 (format as JSON) first, then feed its output backward into Prompt 1 (extract)
Run all three prompts separately on the original raw email thread, ignoring each other's outputs

exercises.explain: A true sequential chain pipes outputs forward in order: extract feeds summarize, summarize feeds format. Each step only sees the transformed result of the step before it, not the raw original text again and not results running backward or independently. This piping is what makes chains reliable — it keeps each prompt focused on one narrow, well-defined job while still building toward the final result.
